channel.title: Yogacara on Early Reflections
channel.description: Recent content in Yogacara on Early Reflections

item.title: The Three Turnings in Classical Yogacara
item.author: sam.earlyreflections@gmail.com (Samuel Ludford)
item.description: One of the key differences between the early Buddhist and later Mahayana teachings is the way they each portray the relationship of the path to its destination. The early teachings stress that in its natural state the mind is obscured by very real afflictions, and the path is accordingly presented as the effort to draw up a map of the afflicted mind and to then use it to systematically cut away the fetters at the root of suffering. Nirvana is achieved only once this active and strenuous process has been completed, a world-transcending state which marks a definitive exit from samsara.
item.content:encoded:
One of the key differences between the early Buddhist and later Mahayana teachings is the way they each portray the relationship of the path to its destination. The early teachings stress that in its natural state the mind is obscured by very real afflictions, and the path is accordingly presented as the effort to draw up a map of the afflicted mind and to then use it to systematically cut away the fetters at the root of suffering. Nirvana is achieved only once this active and strenuous process has been completed, a world-transcending state which marks a definitive exit from samsara.

In contrast, Mahayana traditions tend to stress that the mind is always already awakened, and construe the path as a process of coming to realise this. They are suspicious of modes of practice which treat the afflictions as substantially real, worrying that in doing to so they only reinforce the reifying tendency which inhibits realisation of the Buddha-nature which is already there. Instead they recommend practices based on not-doing, typically non-dual or open awareness practices which aim at a certain ‘abiding in thusness’ without conceptualisation (tantric practices depart from this in some ways, but inhabit the same overall framework). Samsara already is nirvana—your job is to notice that.

The Buddha’s own comments can be ambiguous on this point, as for example when he likens the path to the purification of gold. On the one hand the gold is already there. But on the other hand so are the impurities, and their removal implies a process of real change. How different are these two presentations, really? The question feels live for me, as I currently seem to be converging on a mode of practice which is essentially neo-Theravada insight meditation, but with a view of the Dharma which is informed primarily by Mahayana philosophy. On the one hand I find the detailed and systematic approach of insight meditation generates enthusiasm and curiosity in me in way that non-dual practices simply do not, at least at this stage of my practice. Even if you accept that the aim is to stop doing something, rather than to do something, you still need to have a grasp on what exactly it is that you’re aiming to stop doing. Or to use another of the Buddha’s similes, if the Dharma is likened to a raft then you can accept that the raft is just a tool for getting to the other shore, not an end in itself, while also acknowledging that you do in fact need a raft and can’t just teleport across the river.

Yet at the same time I remain sceptical of world-transcending interpretations of nirvana, and do agree that this model generates meta-ethical problems that can’t be resolved without embracing a more thorough-going philosophy of emptiness and something like the Bodhisattva ideal (though I’d perhaps add the caveat that exactly how these innovations solve those problems is far less obvious than generally acknowledged).

Squaring this circle has been one of the main factors driving me to explore Yogacara more deeply. Following on from Ben Connelley’s book on Vasubandhu’s Thirty Verses, which I discussed previously, I’ve been reading William Waldron’s superb Making Sense of Mind-Only [1], which is significantly more comprehensive and historical in scope. Like Connelly, Waldron rejects the interpretation of classical Yogacara (which includes the Yogacara sutras along with the classic expositions of Vasubandhu and Asanga in the 4th or 5th century) as a metaphysical idealism. Instead he sees it as a form of cognitive constructivism, more concerned with how experience arises as a function of the interaction between our environment and our mental faculties than with fundamental ontology.

Of particular interest here is the Yogacara teaching on the “three turnings of the wheel of Dharma,” a framework through which Yogacara synthesises the detailed psychological maps of earlier Buddhism with the new Mahayana emptiness teachings found in the Perfection of Wisdom sutras and their explication by Nagarjuna. The first turning refers to both the early Buddhist teachings as presented in the Pali discourses, and their systematisation in the various Abhidharma schools that followed.

According to the Abhidharma analysis all experience can be broken down into constitutive elements called (somewhat confusingly) dharmas, point-like moments of awareness constantly flashing in and out of existence. All the normal objects of our experience—medium-sized dry goods, thoughts, sensations, our sense of self—are just higher-level patternings of these momentary dharmas, appearing with a false solidity in roughly the same way that moving objects appear on a film screen despite being constituted by a rapid sequence of static frames. One of the features of many Abhidharma systems is that the dharmas are the only entities considered to ultimately exist, while everything else exists only in some derivative sense.

The second turning refers to the Mahayana emptiness teachings, and its distinctive innovation is that it rejects the ultimate reality of dharmas. A consequence of this stance is that it flattens the difference in status between the dharmas and aggregate phenomena, which are now all viewed as equally empty of essence or substantial reality. One lesson that could be taken from this, which later comes to define the Madhyamaka school, is that Buddhist theorists should stop trying to construct positive accounts of the functioning of the mind and reorient themselves toward the deconstruction of any and all views that treat—explicitly or implicitly—some entity or class of entities as non-empty.

While Madhyamaka could be described as an anti-metaphysics, it remains in a metaphysical register in the sense that its purely negative style of reasoning aims to deconstruct the fallacy of reification that underlies all positive metaphysical views, a fallacy it takes to be the primary barrier to liberation. In light of this it is perhaps not surprising that Madhyamaka philosophers like Candrakirti, writing several hundred years after Asanga and Vasubandhu, often construed Yogacara as a metaphysical idealism only to then accuse it of reifying the mind.

Waldron argues that Yogacara is better seen as a different kind of response to the emptiness teachings, one which can first be approached by considering the limitations of Madhyamaka. In restricting itself to purely deconstructive discourse, Madhyamaka deprives itself of the resources needed to answer certain kinds of questions. For example, Madhyamaka agrees that there is such a thing as conventional truth and hence that there is a difference between conventional truth and conventional falsehood. But at the same time it seems to preclude the possiblity of our ever being able to say what this difference consists in. The Madhyamika seems committed to a kind of quietism about the nature of conventional truth—the best they could say is that if you want to know what mathematical truth is, ask a mathematician; if you want to know what historical truth is, ask a historian; but there is no way of getting at an overarching, domain-neutral theory of conventional truth which the univocity of the concept seems to demand. This is not inconsistent, but it is certainly unsatisfying. The Madhyamika might say that the difference is just that some conventional beliefs are useful while others are not, but a statement like that remains vacuous in the absence of an account of what it is that might make a conventional belief useful in general. And it is exactly that kind of account that Madhyamaka forbids itself from offering.

Furthermore, from the point of view of liberation the issue is not really that we believe phenomena to non-empty. The issue is that we perceive them to be so, and this may be the case regardless of what we believe. If we are to free ourselves of our ingrained perception of the world as infused with illusory essence, what we’d really like is a substantial account of why it is that our mental faculties automatically impute essences to phenomena which do not in fact possess them. Once again, here Madhyamaka can only maintain a principled silence. But for Yogacara this is the key question, and what allows it to take it up as a positive program is precisely its reframing of the emptiness teachings in cognitive rather than metaphysical terms. This is the move which defines the third turning of the wheel of Dharma.

For Yogacarins, then, the relationship to the Abhidharma looks like this. As per the emptiness teachings they reject the ultimate reality of the dharmas, but they do not take this as reason to reject the Abhidharma framework as a whole. Instead they reinterpret the dharmas as something more like epistemological primitives than ontological primitives—as building blocks of a theory of mind rather than building blocks of reality. This allows them to use those aspects of the Abhidharma which successfully explain our mental patterns while augmenting them with extra elements which could help to explain those aspects of mind which the Abhidharma system struggled to accommodate. For instance in another of its signature teachings, Yogacara posits an unconscious layer of mental processing to account for memory and karmic continuity, neither of which can easily be made sense of in a system in which the only components are momentary, sequential, and manifestly present dharmas.

What all this points to is a way of integrating the Mahayana emptiness teachings with a form of practice that is continuous with, rather than divergent from, insight practices rooted in the substantial content of the first turning psychology. Without seeing these as descriptions of substantially real afflictions one can nevertheless actively engage them as necessary tools—as in the simile of the raft—in coming to see exactly how the illusion of substantiality is staged, and which can be discarded once they have served their purpose.

item.title: Yogacara's Idealism
item.author: sam.earlyreflections@gmail.com (Samuel Ludford)
item.description: I’ve just finished Ben Connelly’s book Inside Vasubandhu’s Yogacara [1], which while primarily a practice guide also doubles as a rather good primer on Yogacara philosophy. Yogacara can feel particularly opaque, and understanding which aspects are psychological and which are more philosophical, what those philosophical aspects are trying to say, which bits are minor modifications of the Abhidharma framework and which are genuine innovations, if or how it disagrees with other Buddhist philosophical schools, and so on, can be tricky to say the least. But for the purposes this post I’m going to put all that aside and focus on the key Yogacara doctrine of ‘mind-only’.
item.content:encoded:
I’ve just finished Ben Connelly’s book Inside Vasubandhu’s Yogacara [1], which while primarily a practice guide also doubles as a rather good primer on Yogacara philosophy. Yogacara can feel particularly opaque, and understanding which aspects are psychological and which are more philosophical, what those philosophical aspects are trying to say, which bits are minor modifications of the Abhidharma framework and which are genuine innovations, if or how it disagrees with other Buddhist philosophical schools, and so on, can be tricky to say the least. But for the purposes this post I’m going to put all that aside and focus on the key Yogacara doctrine of ‘mind-only’.

Mind-only has precedents in the early Buddhist texts—most famously in the Dhammapada, which opens with the words “Mind precedes all mental states. Mind is their chief; they are all mind-wrought.”—but its development in and centrality to Yogacara has led it to be described as a Buddhist idealism, and much of the discussion around Yogacara aims to clarify what kind of idealism it is. One prominent current construes Yogacara as a kind of metaphysical idealism, claiming that mental states are all that ultimately exist. Going against this current, Connelly gives an epistemological reading of the mind-only doctrine as saying that nothing in our experience escapes conceptual mediation (a claim which is non-committal with regard to how things actually are).

To make a comparison with Western forms of idealism, Connelly’s reading places Yogacara much closer to the transcendental idealism of Kant than to the subjective idealism of Berkeley. And if this comparison of Yogacara with Kant is even halfway right, it should warn us that it will likely generate all sorts of confusion. One of the worries often leveled against epistemological idealism is that it fails to define a substantial position distinct from metaphysical idealism, i.e. that it either says something too trivial to be substantial, or that insofar as it says something substantial it does so only by smuggling in metaphysical commitments. This in mind, it’s worth unpacking some of the implications to get a better grasp on what it’s actually saying.

In early modern European philosophy it was common to make a distinction between perceptible properties—like colour and smell—which are mediated (or conditioned) by our perceptual systems, and those—like shape, position in space and time, unity, etc—which belong to the nature of the object itself, irrespective of the mediating contribution of our own mind. Kant rejected this picture by arguing that there are no perceptible properties which are not mediated by our own mental faculties, including resolutely ‘objective’ properties like shape, time, and space. Many have since wondered how metaphysical this kind of view is. If space and time are transcendentally ideal in the sense that they represent the conceptual activity of our mind in structuring experience, does this imply that what cosmologists are studying is really just the contents of our own minds? If so, this really would seem to commit Kant to a kind of metaphysical idealism, and a somewhat implausible one at that.

But there’s another way of reading Kant. On this reading, which deflates the metaphysical scope of his idealism, the fact that nothing in our experience escapes conceptual mediation is just a banal consequence of the fact that concepts are how we get a grip on the world. If an elephant experiences the world by grasping it with its trunk, then it is correct to say that everything in its experience is mediated by the contributing structure of its trunk. But it would be wrong to then conclude from this that it does not grasp the real world at all. And it is the same, the transcendental idealist says, with the way our experience is structured by our faculty of conceptual understanding.

This is the kind of interpretation which is sometimes accused of triviality, but it has radical implications. What it amounts to is a wholesale rejection of a certain model of cognition which in the West is associated most strongly with Descartes. According to this Cartesian model our experience of the world is mediated by mental representations, which taken together form the inner world of consciousness. We stand in an unmediated relation with the mental representations themselves, and then there is a secondary matter of how accurately these representations on the screen of consciousness mirror the real world. The direct objects of our experience are mental representations, and our experience of these is unmediated. In this sense these primary experiences are infallible—you can’t be wrong about the way things seem to you. The mediation (and hence fallibility) is only introduced at the second stage. Kant rejects this two-stage picture, instead taking the view that when you experience a tree, say, you are not having an unmediated experience of a mental representation of a tree (which may or may not accurately represent a real tree). You are simply having a conceptually mediated experience of the tree.

If this distinction feels obscure, it can be illustrated with a simple example that will also help bring this back to Yogacara. Say you’re walking home at night and are startled by what seems to be a person lurking at the side of the road, but on second glance you realise it is a tree swaying in the wind. The Kantian has a very simple story about what has happened here—they say that you saw a tree and misapplied the concept ‘person’. But for the Cartesian a question arises about what mental representation was present when the erroneous judgement was made. If they say that there was a mental representation of a tree which was misinterpreted as a person, then this violates the idea that we have unmediated access to our conscious experience. But if they say that there was a mental representation of a person, as it seems they will have to, then this opens the door to a generalised scepticism. Because if such a disjunction between mental representation and object represented is possible, then how can we know our mental representations ever accurately represent the world at all?

The Yogacarin’s picture looks very similar to the Kantian’s. They would say both that your experience of the real tree is mediated by karmic impressions residing in the ‘store consciousness’1 (in this case the combination of evolutionary, cultural, and perhaps personal conditioning which sets you up to see an unexpected tree as a menacing figure), and that there’s no way of stripping away this mediation to get at the raw or ‘true’ experience. This is true both in the case of erroneous experience as in the example, and in normal veridical experience. Indeed, mind-only is the idea that karmic mediation is constitutive of our conscious experience in general.

Put this way the position I am attributing to Kant and the Yogacarin may seem reasonable, but the Cartesian intuition it runs counter to—that there is some intrinsic way that things seem to us prior to our mind’s conceptual framing of those things—is hard to shake. We tend to think there is some way a particular shade of grey looks, and are surprised by optical illusions in which the same shade of grey appears different depending on its surrounding shades. We struggle to get our heads round the fact that a C major scale and an A minor scale—two quite different tonalities—contain exactly the same notes. We believe that we can see colour in our peripheral vision, but under experimental conditions perfectly well-sighted humans prove useless at distinguishing even vivid colours at the edge of their visual field. Afflictions like Anton syndrome—in which a person who is anatomically blind, who has no activity in their optical nerve and will bump into obstacles and fail to catch a ball when thrown at them, will nevertheless insist that they can see—trouble us at a philosophical level. We want to know what it is these people are actually experiencing, yet it seems hard to say what that might be or even what this question might mean.

The mind-only view that nothing in experience escapes conceptual mediation also has implications for how we think about insight experiences and awakening. As intimated earlier, if there is no unmediated experience then all experience is fallible. In particular there is no special category of ‘direct’ experience (of emptiness, thusness, Buddha-nature, etc) that is intrinsically truth-revealing.

In the Thirty Verses on Mind-Only—the classic Yogacara text by 4th/5th Century Buddhist philosopher Vasubandhu that Connelly’s book is based on—there’s a striking example of this when Vasubandhu asserts that the other-dependent nature of a phenomenon, which is its ultimate truth as empty or dependently arisen, is itself a conceptualisation. This is an idea which goes against the grain of many framings of awakening, particularly those which lean on a metaphysical interpretation of mind-only. Among these it’s not uncommon to find the following story: consciousness is fundamental to reality, and conceptualisation is an extra layer added by our minds to the raw experiential flux, including the conceptualisation that separates reality into self and other. On this view awakening is about lifting this veil of conceptualisation to come into a direct (i.e. unmediated) contact with pure, unconceptualised consciousness, which is also ultimate reality. This is precisely the kind of model rejected by Connelly’s Vasubandhu. For Yogacara so understood, awakening is not about going beyond conceptual mediation but rather ‘resting in mind-only’—the recognition of the conceptually mediated nature of experienced reality as conceptually mediated, together with the recognition that there is nothing beyond mediation.

  1. The store consciousness (alaya-vijnana) is another key Yogacara concept, describing an unconscious layer of the mind where karmic impressions are ‘stored’. ↩︎
